synthesis_text: 1. Monitor system metrics (CPU, memory, network) before and after the release to spot regressions (N1:7:5b71ff31). 2. Collect gateway error timestamps and match them to backend logs via request IDs to locate failing paths (N1:1:80f5b1d1). 3. Instrument the application to measure latency of each downstream call versus DB calls, isolating the slowest component (N2:5:20c254b2). 4. Enable distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to capture per‑call latency and failure rates across services (N6:1:9c8f589f). 5. Review and raise gateway and backend timeout values to exceed the worst‑case downstream latency (N3:6:34740872). 6. Profile database CPU and query execution plans; identify top‑CPU queries, missing indexes, or lock‑wait issues (N1:3:56031488, N7:0:e3ef8b38). 7. Check DB connection‑pool usage and size; ensure the pool isn’t exhausted causing timeouts (N1:4:0039e416). 8. If the issue appeared after a recent release, rollback or feature‑flag the change to confirm the source (N6:6:091912cd). 9. Capture packets between the gateway and services to detect loss, high latency, or retransmission bursts that could trigger 502/504 (N8:4:2e8279c8). 10. Add timeouts and circuit‑breaker thresholds around downstream calls and implement exponential backoff retries to fail fast and prevent cascading errors (N1:5:ceffd77b).

To pinpoint the cause of the intermittent 502/504 errors, first collect gateway logs and match error timestamps with backend request IDs (N1:1:80f5b1d1, N6:0:be42ea7d). Enable request‑level tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) on the gateway and backend to correlate each request with downstream latency (N1:0:73b6996b, N6:1:9c8f589f). Use the tracing data to identify which downstream call or DB query exceeds its SLA. On the database side, activate pg_stat_statements and DB latency/lock‑wait metrics to surface the top CPU‑intensive queries (N2:0:84193441, N7:0:e3ef8b38), then inspect their execution plans for full scans or missing indexes (N7:1:3655c323) and add appropriate indexes (N2:2:61351358). Reduce contention by limiting parallelism or batching DB requests (N6:5:2ab64944). With the latency profile, budget the total request timeout by allocating time slices to each downstream call plus a safety margin (N10:4:c70e96aa) and enforce timeouts and circuit‑breaker thresholds around those calls to fail fast and prevent cascading failures (N1:5:ceffd77b). This systematic tracing, DB query optimization, and timeout/breaker tuning will isolate the bottleneck and stabilize the system.

First, capture system‑level metrics before and after the release to spot regressions (N1:7:5b71ff31). Collect gateway error logs with timestamps and match them to backend logs using request IDs (N1:1:80f5b1d1, N6:0:be42ea7d) and enable request‑level tracing such as OpenTelemetry to see which downstream call exceeds its budget (N1:0:73b6996b). Monitor database CPU and profile its workload (N1:3:56031488) while enabling pg_stat_statements to identify the hottest queries (N2:0:84193441). Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on those queries (N2:1:ed78d84d) and add any missing indexes (N2:2:61351358). If the DB is saturated, limit parallelism or batch requests to reduce contention (N6:5:2ab64944) and consider rolling back or feature‑flagging recent changes to isolate the regression (N6:6:091912cd).

1. Collect gateway error logs with timestamps and correlate them to backend request IDs (N1:1:80f5b1d1). 2. Enable request‑level tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to see which downstream call exceeds latency budgets (N1:0:73b6996b). 3. Measure end‑to‑end latency and compare it against gateway and backend timeout settings; adjust timeouts to exceed the worst‑case downstream latency (N3:6:34740872). 4. Monitor backend CPU, memory and thread‑pool usage; high DB CPU can delay responses (N3:4:4d146200). 5. Enable pg_stat_statements to identify the top CPU‑consuming queries (N2:0:84193441) and run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to spot missing indexes (N2:2:61351358). 6. Tune the DB connection‑pool size to prevent saturation (N2:4:e9698800). 7. Add explicit timeouts and circuit‑breaker thresholds around downstream calls so failures fail fast and do not cascade (N1:5:ceffd77b). 8. Verify load‑balancer health checks are passing; failing checks can produce 502 errors (N3:0:c7b2b1e0). 9. Combine the above analysis to decide whether to add indexes, increase pool size, or scale the database/read replicas to reduce CPU pressure (N6:4:6ff1c8f5).

Begin by rolling back or feature‑flagging the recent release to confirm it introduced the issue (N6:6:091912cd). Collect gateway 502/504 timestamps and correlate them with backend request IDs (N1:1:80f5b1d1) while enabling request‑level tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to map each request to downstream calls (N1:0:73b6996b). Measure gateway latency distribution (p95/p99) and error rates to establish a baseline (N10:0:5a9779c2). Instrument the application to separate latency of downstream service calls from database calls (N2:5:20c254b2) and enable pg_stat_statements to identify the top CPU‑consuming queries (N2:0:84193441). Activate DB latency and lock‑wait metrics (N7:0:e3ef8b38) and inspect execution plans of hot queries for full scans or missing indexes (N7:1:3655c323). If queries are inefficient, add missing indexes or tune them, and adjust the DB connection‑pool size to avoid saturation (N7:4:feb4eb86). Profile downstream services for spikes and introduce circuit‑breakers or rate‑limiting to fail fast (N7:4:feb4eb86). Finally, if downstream latency cannot be reduced, increase the gateway timeout or adjust its retry policy (N6:8:796e6a89).

Begin by collecting gateway error logs (N1:1:80f5b1d1) and enabling request‑level tracing (N1:0:73b6996b) to map 502/504 timestamps to specific backend request IDs and downstream latency. Correlate these with system‑wide metrics (CPU, memory, network) taken before and after the release (N1:7:5b71ff31) to confirm the database CPU spike. Inspect the DB connection pool usage and query execution times (N3:5:9167f4ff); if the pool is saturated, increase its size or batch requests. Review execution plans of the hot queries for full scans or missing indexes (N7:1:3655c323) and add the needed indexes (N2:2:61351358) to reduce CPU load. Limit parallelism or batch database calls (N6:5:2ab64944) to further lower contention. Verify that gateway and backend timeout settings exceed the worst‑case downstream latency (N3:6:34740872) and apply a timeout budget that allocates time slices to each downstream call plus a safety margin (N10:4:c70e96aa). Finally, add circuit breakers around slow downstream services (N6:3:506316a5) to fail fast and prevent cascading timeouts, then re‑run load‑tests to validate that the adjustments eliminate the intermittent failures.
